skill.name: native-feel-cross-platform-desktop
skill.description: Use when the user is designing, prototyping, or rewriting a desktop app that must run on multiple OSes (macOS + Windows, optionally Linux) AND feel indistinguishable from a native app to its users — fast launch, native windowing, native input handling, native materials. Trigger words include "cross-platform desktop", "Electron alternative", "Tauri vs native", "WebView wrapper", "near-native performance", "Raycast architecture", "WebKit/WebView2 quirks", "WKWebView", "system tray app", "global hotkey app", "launcher app". Do NOT trigger this skill for pure web apps, pure mobile apps, or for greenfield projects that have no native-feel requirement.
skill.summary: Architectural reference for building cross-platform desktop apps that feel native on macOS and Windows using a four-layer shell, WebView, Node, and Rust pattern.
skill.author: yetone

security_audit.summary: This is an educational reference skill containing markdown documentation about cross-platform desktop app architecture. All 508 static analysis findings are false positives in their full context. The 382 'external_commands' detections are shell commands shown inside code blocks as educational examples. The 'keylogger keywords', 'Windows SAM database', and 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are due to the scanner matching words like 'keystroke', 'DwmSetWindowAttribute' (DWM compositor API, not SAM), and 'crypto' (as a general subsystem category) in architectural discussion text. No executable code, network exfiltration, or credential access exists in this skill.

low_findings.title: Shell commands in educational code blocks
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: 382 instances of shell commands detected in backtick code blocks across markdown files. All instances are educational documentation examples showing macOS diagnostic commands (vm_stat, vmmap, heap), Swift configuration, and Node.js commands. No commands are executed. This is a reference skill showing development patterns.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation
low_findings.description: TRUE BUT BENIGN: 9 instances of hardcoded URLs detected. All are legitimate documentation references: GitHub repository URLs, license badge images, and a link to the Raycast technical blog post. No data exfiltration or malicious network activity exists.

low_findings.title: Home directory reference in install instructions
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Reference to ~/.claude/skills/ at README.md:28 is the standard installation path for Claude Code skills. This is documentation showing how to install the skill, not unauthorized filesystem access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static heuristic: code execution + network + credential access combination
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static analyzer flagged a heuristic combination at the skill level. In context, this is an educational reference skill discussing cross-platform desktop architecture. References to 'crypto' appear in discussions about performance-critical subsystems (SKILL.md:25). References to shell commands are in markdown code blocks as documentation. All 'network' findings are documentation links. No actual code execution, network access, or credential handling occurs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Windows DWM API confused with SAM database
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged 'Windows SAM database' at references/03-webview-survival.md:300 and references/06-native-conventions.md:36. Both locations reference DwmSetWindowAttribute, the Windows Desktop Window Manager API used for acrylic/mica visual effects on window backgrounds. This is a standard Windows compositor API, not the Security Account Manager database.

dangerous_patterns.title: Keystroke reference in performance discussion
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The scanner flagged 'keylogger keywords' at references/01-philosophy.md:54,72. Line 54 uses 'keystroke' in the context of defining performance perception targets. Line 72 uses 'keystroke' in discussing IPC performance boundaries. Both are legitimate architectural discussions about app responsiveness, not keylogger functionality.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment file entries in .gitignore
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Entries for .env and .env.local in .gitignore:6-7 are standard security best practice to prevent accidental commit of environment variables. This is protective, not a vulnerability.

content.user_title: Build Cross-Platform Desktop Apps with Native Feel
content.value_statement: Building cross-platform desktop apps that feel native on macOS and Windows is difficult. This skill provides architectural guidance, WebView optimization techniques, and a 75-item ship-readiness checklist to help you build desktop apps that users cannot distinguish from native software.
actual_capabilities: Guide architecture decisions for cross-platform desktop apps using a four-layer native shell pattern
Provide WebView survival techniques for WKWebView and WebView2 with real bug fixes from production apps
Run a 75-item ship-readiness audit to verify native feel across input, motion, materials, and keyboard behaviors
Design typed IPC contracts across Rust, Swift, C#, and TypeScript runtimes
Analyze memory usage honestly by distinguishing baseline costs from margin costs
limitations: Cannot write or modify actual source code for your project
Does not cover mobile app development or React Native patterns
Architecture guidance is grounded in macOS and Windows evidence; Linux coverage is partial
Cannot replace the need for experienced native platform engineers on your team

use_cases.title: Refactoring an Existing Electron App for Better Native Feel
use_cases.description: An engineering lead with an Electron app that feels web-like wants concrete steps to improve native feel without a full rewrite. The skill provides the ship-readiness checklist and targeted WebView fixes.

use_cases.title: Architecting a New Cross-Platform Desktop App from Scratch
use_cases.description: A CTO planning a new productivity tool needs architectural guidance on native shell, WebView, and Rust core layering decisions. The skill provides the decision tree and four-layer architecture reference.

use_cases.title: Debugging WebView Performance Issues
use_cases.description: A frontend developer facing WKWebView flickering or Chromium throttling needs specific fixes and configuration flags for the system WebView. The skill provides the WebView survival guide with documented bugs and fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Initial Architecture Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: I am planning a new cross-platform desktop app that needs to feel native on macOS and Windows. Should I use the four-layer architecture described in this skill? Run the decision tree in checklists/decision-tree.md and advise me.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with cross-platform desktop architecture

prompt_templates.title: WebView Bug Diagnosis
prompt_templates.prompt: My WKWebView flickers when I hide and re-show the window on macOS. Walk me through the fixes in references/03-webview-survival.md and help me identify which section applies to my issue.

prompt_templates.title: IPC Contract Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to design typed IPC between my Rust core, Swift native shell, and TypeScript WebView layer. Show me the pattern from references/04-ipc-contract.md and help me model my first two message types using UniFFI.

prompt_templates.title: Pre-Launch Native Feel Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full 75-item ship-readiness checklist from checklists/ship-readiness.md against my app. For each failing item, explain the exact fix and prioritize them by user impact.
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing a cross-platform app for launch readiness

output_examples.input: My Electron app feels web-y. Where do I start?
output_examples.output: The checklist in checklists/ship-readiness.md covers 75 items. Most Electron apps fail the same 6-8 items. The quickest fixes include removing cursor:pointer on rows, disabling WebKit context menus, using native system accent colors instead of brand colors, and replacing web-style modal overlays with native sheets. Each fix takes 5-30 minutes. For deeper native feel, you need the four-layer architecture with a native shell owning the window and materials.

output_examples.input: Activity Monitor says my app uses 450 MB. Is that bad?
output_examples.output: This matches the expected baseline for a native shell plus WebView plus Node backend architecture. Activity Monitor double-counts shared frameworks and treats compressed pages as resident. Before optimizing, separate baseline costs (WebView at 50MB, Node at 12MB) from margin costs (your code). Use vm_stat or Instruments footprint tool for accurate measurement.

best_practices: Start each session by running the decision tree in checklists/decision-tree.md to verify the architecture fits the project
Reference specific tenets from references/01-philosophy.md by number and short name when giving architectural advice
Always separate baseline costs from margin costs before optimizing memory or performance
anti_patterns: Recommending the four-layer architecture without first running the decision tree to rule out simpler alternatives like Electron or pure native
Treating the skill as a code generator instead of a reference for architectural guidance and debugging
Applying web UI patterns such as cursor:pointer on rows or modal overlays on top of the native shell, which negates native feel

faq.question: What tools does this skill work with?
faq.answer: This skill works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as a knowledge reference for cross-platform desktop architecture guidance.

faq.question: Can this skill generate code for my app?
faq.answer: No. This skill is a reference for architectural decisions, WebView debugging, and native-feel checklists. It does not generate or modify source code.

faq.question: Does this skill cover mobile app development?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on macOS and Windows desktop apps. Mobile development with React Native or SwiftUI is not covered.

faq.question: Is the four-layer architecture the only option for cross-platform desktop apps?
faq.answer: No. The decision tree in checklists/decision-tree.md helps evaluate when Electron, Tauri, or pure native development are better choices for your project.

faq.question: How is this skill different from Electron?
faq.answer: This skill describes a native shell architecture where the operating system owns the window and materials, and the WebView is only a rendering surface. Electron abstracts these platform APIs away.

faq.question: Does this skill contain actual security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. This is an educational reference skill containing markdown documentation. Code examples shown in backtick blocks are illustrative patterns for developers, not executable code.
